pattern: Scanning right endpoints while tracking the closest forbidden left boundary -- and, separately, what that boundary would fall back to if a single specific pair were absent -- turns removing the most disruptive pair into a running credit each position hands to whichever pair is currently responsible for it.
hints: For a fixed set of forbidden pairs, the number of valid subarrays ending at each right endpoint depends only on the closest forbidden left-position seen so far -- summing that over every right endpoint gives the total count without the removal.

As you scan endpoints left to right, keep track not just of the current closest forbidden boundary but also of what the boundary would become if the single pair currently enforcing it were taken away.

Whenever exactly one pair is solely responsible for the current boundary, credit that pair with the gap between the current boundary and its fallback; the pair with the largest total credit is the one worth removing.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, removing the first pair helps most
statement example, three pairs sharing an endpoint
smallest array with a single pair
duplicate identical pairs, removing one still leaves the constraint active
two pairs that never both bind the same right endpoint at once
many pairs share the same restrictive left endpoint
pairs given with the larger index first

stress.description: random small n with many random pairs, exercising ties and overlaps
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 500))
    m = rng.randint(1, min(2 * n, 800))
    pairs = []
    for _ in range(m):
        a = rng.randint(1, n)
        b = rng.randint(1, n)
        while b == a:
            b = rng.randint(1, n)
        pairs.append([a, b])
    return [n, pairs]
